channel.title: Category-Theory on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Category-Theory on emsenn.net

item.title: Formalizing Sign Theory: A Survey of Mathematical Approaches to Semiotics
item.description:
This survey examines how researchers have used mathematical structures — particularly category theory, algebra, and topos theory — to formalize aspects of semiotics. The goal is not historical completeness but assessment: for each approach, what does the formalization capture about sign processes, and what does it miss?

The survey serves a specific purpose within this vault. The semiotic universe specification constructs a formal system whose components — a Heyting algebra, modal closure, trace comonad, typed lambda calculus, closure operators — correspond to aspects of Peircean semiosis. Understanding how other formalizations have approached the same material clarifies what the semiotic universe construction does that these approaches do not, and where it might learn from them.

item.title: Monads and Comonads
item.description:
¶Entry conditions

Use monads and comonads when you can:

  • Start from a category (or a poset viewed as a thin category).
  • Identify an endofunctor T : C -> C that “wraps” or “annotates” objects.
  • Ask whether the wrapping is coherent: can it be applied once, twice, or unwrapped, in ways that compose consistently?

¶Definitions

A monad on a category C is a triple (T, eta, mu) where:

item.title: Enriched Categories
item.description:
An enriched category replaces hom-sets with hom-objects from a fixed monoidal category.

¶Data

An enriched category over a monoidal category V has:

  • A class of objects.
  • For each pair of objects (x, y), a hom-object V(x, y) in V.
  • Composition maps V(y, z) tensor V(x, y) -> V(x, z).
  • Unit maps I -> V(x, x).

¶Why this matters

Enrichment lets morphisms carry structure such as topology, probability, or order, instead of being just elements of a set.

item.title: Monoidal Categories
item.description:
A monoidal category is a category equipped with extra structure that lets you combine objects in a coherent way.

¶Data

A monoidal category consists of:

  • A category C.
  • A tensor product operation tensor : C x C -> C.
  • A unit object I.
  • Natural isomorphisms that express associativity and unit laws.

¶Coherence idea

The associativity and unit constraints ensure that different parenthesizations lead to the same result, so composition in enriched categories is well-defined.

item.title: Quasi-Categories
item.description:
A quasi-category is a simplicial set in which every inner horn has a filler. This condition ensures that higher composition exists up to coherent homotopy.

¶Intuition

  • Vertices represent objects.
  • Edges represent 1-morphisms.
  • Higher simplices encode higher morphisms and their coherence data.

Quasi-categories are a widely used model for infinity categories because they translate higher categorical structure into combinatorial data.

item.title: Why Infinity Categories
item.description:
Ordinary categories capture objects and morphisms, but many constructions in homotopy theory require higher morphisms that witness equivalences.

Infinity categories add morphisms in every dimension so that composition and equivalence are tracked up to higher homotopies, not strict equality.

This gives a setting where the objects of interest are equivalent in a flexible way while still supporting categorical reasoning.
